sources.notes: Read the complete damage model, fixed-integer-position restriction, constraints, impossibility condition, and all sample explanations.

Read the official 2037F analysis and Python code. It binary-searches the attack count, derives one valid-position interval per enemy using ceil(h_i/q), and checks whether at least k intervals overlap by a sorted event sweep.

Read the full independent explanation and implementation. It confirms the inclusive interval endpoints and event tie handling. Its stated O(n log V) complexity omits the event-sorting factor present in its own code, so the recorded complexity follows the actual implementation and official analysis.

Re-derived monotonicity, the 1e9 upper bound, and inclusive interval events. Exhaustively compared the interval algorithm with brute-force enumeration of integer positions and attack counts on small instances; all results matched.

reference_candidates.notes: The contest status page reports verdict Accepted, but the source page was blocked by browser verification, so its code was not consulted or used as evidence.

annotation.summary: Choose one fixed integer position p and minimize the number of identical attacks needed to defeat at least k enemies, where per-hit damage to enemy i is max(0,m-|p-x_i|). Return -1 if no fixed position can ever damage k enemies.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 10^4; 1 <= k <= n <= 10^5; 1 <= m,h_i,x_i <= 10^9; positions are strictly increasing; the sum of n over all test cases is at most 10^5.
annotation.core_idea: Binary-search a candidate attack count q. Enemy i dies within q attacks exactly when its per-hit damage is at least need_i=ceil(h_i/q). If need_i>m, that enemy is impossible for this q. Otherwise |p-x_i| <= m-need_i, so every suitable integer position belongs to the closed interval [x_i-m+need_i, x_i+m-need_i]. Thus q is feasible exactly when some integer coordinate is covered by at least k such intervals. Add +1 at every left endpoint and -1 at right endpoint plus one, sort the events, and test whether the running coverage reaches k. Increasing q never increases need_i, so intervals only appear or expand and feasibility is monotone. If any solution exists, every selected enemy takes at least one damage per hit and has health at most 1e9, so q=1e9 is feasible; failure there proves the answer is -1.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n log H) time per test case, where H=max(h_i)<=1e9, and O(n) auxiliary memory. Across the input, sum n <= 10^5.
tricks: Binary-search the number of attacks rather than the position.
Convert the damage inequality into a closed interval of legal positions for each enemy.
Use an event at R+1 so enemies whose legal intervals meet at one endpoint are counted together.
Use q=1e9 as both a valid finite upper bound and the impossibility test.

solution_variants.name: attack-count-binary-search-interval-sweep
solution_variants.summary: Binary-search q, turn every enemy killable within q attacks into an inclusive interval of valid fixed positions, and use a sorted difference-event sweep to detect a point covered by at least k intervals.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n log H) time and O(n) memory

tags.evidence: The minimum attack count is found from the monotone predicate that if q attacks can kill at least k enemies from one position, every larger attack count can do so as well.
Each feasibility check sorts interval start and end events and sweeps the coordinate line while maintaining the number of enemies simultaneously killable from the current position.
For fixed q, the damage inequality for each enemy becomes one interval for the shared hidden parameter p, and feasibility asks whether some p lies in at least k of those intervals.
